title: How to Work with Existential Questions That Arise During Journeys
body:
How to Work with Existential Questions That Arise During Journeys

1. Opening Hook - When the Universe Asks You Hard Questions

One evening, on a particularly vivid mushroom journey, I found myself lying on the cool grass beneath an endless sky. The usual swirling colors had faded into a quiet, almost still awareness. Then, out of nowhere, a question struck me with the weight of a cosmic riddle: “What is my purpose in this vast, indifferent universe?” It wasn’t the kind of question you can shake off or tuck away for later. It demanded my full attention, pressing me to confront the very core of my existence. I felt both terrified and strangely curious, like standing at the edge of an immense abyss but knowing that looking away wasn’t an option.

Those moments of raw existential questioning are familiar to many who journey with psychedelics, especially mushrooms. They don’t come as neat, easy puzzles but as deep, sometimes wrenching invitations to wrestle with life’s biggest mysteries: Why am I here? What does it mean to be alive? What happens when I die? These questions emerge not out of intellectual curiosity but from the heart and soul, shaking the foundations of our everyday assumptions.

Personally, I’ve learned that pushing aside these questions is like ignoring a fire alarm. The psychedelic experience tends to amplify our awareness, strip away distractions, and temporarily dissolve the usual filters of ego and routine that usually keep these thoughts at bay. The intensity of these reflections can feel overwhelming, even paralyzing. But if we can meet them with openness instead of fear, the journey shifts from a confusing riddle into a powerful passage toward deeper understanding. The challenge is not only handling these questions while under the influence but integrating their implications long after the experience ends.

Many people come into psychedelic sessions hoping for healing or insight but get blindsided by existential uncertainty. That uncertainty can either become a source of anxiety or a portal to growth, depending on how we respond. And that’s what we’re going to explore here: how to work with these existential questions instead of running from them, how to hold the mystery without losing your mind, and how to gently weave the wisdom you gain into your daily life afterward.

Before we get into practical tools and techniques, it’s worth acknowledging something that James Fadiman and others have highlighted in their work with psychedelics: these experiences are personal and unpredictable. There’s no one-size-fits-all answer or neat formula. But with patience, curiosity, and some grounded strategies, you can build a relationship with those big questions that doesn’t leave you spinning but helps you grow into a wiser, more present version of yourself.

2. Core Concept Explanation with Scientific Backing

Existential questions during psychedelic journeys don’t just arise randomly; they’re linked to the brain’s shifting patterns under the influence of compounds like psilocybin, the active ingredient in many mushrooms. Normally, our brains operate in distinct networks - like the default mode network (DMN) - that manage our sense of self, memories, and the habitual chatter of ego-centered thoughts. Under psilocybin, research shows this network’s activity quiets down dramatically, allowing otherwise separated brain areas to communicate in novel ways.

This disruption leads to what neuroscientists like Robin Carhart-Harris have called “ego dissolution,” where the boundaries between self and world blur. With those boundaries dissolving, the mind often shifts away from mundane concerns and toward deeper, underlying questions about meaning, existence, and connection. Rather than floating aimlessly, many report feeling “connected to everything” or facing the “big questions” of life without their usual mental filters. The brain becomes a kind of vast open space where long-suppressed or previously unacknowledged thoughts and feelings can surface.

But why existential questions specifically? It’s thought that the decreased activity in the DMN reduces the brain’s noise - the constant self-referential loop - letting the mind see beyond conventional narratives about who we are and our place in the universe. This state is similar to what many spiritual traditions describe as mystical experiences, where the ego feels less distinct and the experience of unity or timelessness is common.

At the same time, the brain’s neuroplasticity is heightened. Increased connectivity and flexibility mean that the mind is more open to new perspectives, including existential reflections that challenge old beliefs or fears. This tendency aligns with Stanislav Grof’s concept of the “holotropic” state, where the psyche moves toward healing by confronting and integrating primal experiences and fundamental truths.

When faced with these questions during a journey, the brain is essentially working through a reorganization of meaning. That can feel disorienting because it shakes up our usual sense of certainty. But it also means the moment contains enormous potential for insight and transformation if approached with care. As Michael Pollan explains in How to Change Your Mind, the psychedelic state allows us to “see ourselves through a wider lens” where existential concerns become more immediate yet more approachable.

Understanding this scientific backdrop helps ground what might otherwise feel like a chaotic or scary experience. It reassures us that the questions we face are part of the brain’s natural response to psychedelic states, a kind of mental re-calibration rather than a breakdown. This perspective is key to not getting lost in the questions but learning to work with them.

3. Research and Evidence - What Studies Tell Us

The last two decades have seen a resurgence in research on psychedelics, with particular focus on psilocybin and its effects on mood, cognition, and personality. Several studies provide insight into how these substances provoke existential questioning and how that process can be beneficial.

One of the most cited studies is the 2006 Johns Hopkins study led by Roland Griffiths. They found that participants who had a “mystical experience” under psilocybin showed significant long-term changes in attitudes about life, death, and meaning. Many described deep shifts in how they understood their purpose and existence, with several reporting lasting improvements in life satisfaction and decreased fear of death. The study’s follow-up interviews revealed that the confrontation with existential questions during the session was a central part of the life-changing process.

Similarly, a 2018 study by Carhart-Harris and colleagues at Imperial College London used brain imaging to track changes during psilocybin therapy for treatment-resistant depression. Participants frequently reported insights relating to self-worth, connection, and existential concerns. The reduction in DMN activity correlated with the depth of these experiences, suggesting a neurological basis for why existential questions come alive during these journeys. Importantly, the study showed that when these questions were processed therapeutically, patients experienced sustained symptom relief, linking existential inquiry to mental health benefits.

Research by Charles Grob and others with ayahuasca - a psychedelic brew often compared to psilocybin in its ability to elicit spiritual and existential experiences - also underscores this. Grob’s work found that participants who worked through existential themes during their sessions often reported increased psychological integration months later, enhancing their sense of meaning and purpose.

What about adverse effects? The Johns Hopkins group also noted that confronting existential questions can sometimes trigger anxiety or distress during and after the session if not properly supported. That finding has led to the emphasis on set and setting - the preparation and aftercare - to help people move through those difficult moments.

These studies collectively suggest that existential questions during psychedelic use are neither accidental nor random but essential elements of the healing and insight process. They also highlight the importance of guidance and integration to help people make sense of these deep experiences, turning what might initially feel like confusion or fear into lasting growth.

4. Practical Application - How You Can Work With Existential Questions

When existential questions arise during your journey, the first step is to accept their presence without resistance. Fighting or suppressing these thoughts often amplifies anxiety. Instead, treat them like a guest who’s come with something important to say. I’ve found that simply sitting with the question, even silently, can be a radical act of courage.

One practice I recommend is journaling immediately after the session or during moments of clarity. Writing down the questions and your initial impressions helps externalize the thoughts and prevent them from spinning endlessly in your mind. If you want a beautiful place to start, I highly recommend a dedicated space like this Journaling Notebook Leather Bound. Having a physical, tactile journal makes the process feel intentional and sacred, creating a container for your reflections.

During the session itself, grounding techniques can be helpful. If a question becomes overwhelming, focus on your breath or your body’s sensations. Drawing on somatic awareness stabilizes you in the present moment and prevents your mind from spiraling into abstract fear. This idea echoes the work of Bessel van der Kolk in The Body Keeps the Score, where he emphasizes the importance of body awareness for processing trauma and intense emotions.

Another method is dialogue - not with others necessarily, but with yourself. You can imagine having a conversation with the question, asking it why it has appeared now. Such inner dialogue turns abstract questions into personal teachers rather than faceless threats.

It’s also okay to embrace ambiguity. Sometimes the question won’t have a neat answer, and that’s part of the experience. I often remind myself that living with uncertainty is a human condition, and learning to befriend it is a form of wisdom. You don’t have to solve the mystery immediately; letting the question simmer can open new insights over time.

Lastly, working with a guide or therapist familiar with psychedelic experiences can make a huge difference. They can hold space for you to explore these big questions safely, helping you avoid getting stuck or overwhelmed. If you don’t have access to professional support, trusted friends who understand the journey can also be invaluable companions.

5. Common Challenges and How to Work Through Them

Existential questions can trigger a powerful mix of fear, confusion, and vulnerability, especially when they touch on mortality, purpose, or identity. One of the most common challenges is the feeling of being utterly lost or nihilistic - wondering if life has any meaning at all. That can spiral into despair if left unchecked.

When this happens, grounding yourself in simple, immediate experiences can help. Returning attention to your breath, the sounds around you, or the sensation of your feet on the ground reminds you that you’re anchored in the present, even if your mind is wandering vast cosmic spaces. Techniques like mindfulness meditation or gentle yoga postures can be especially useful here.

Another frequent hurdle is the temptation to intellectualize or rationalize the questions, trying to force answers too quickly. Psychedelic states often defy logic and linear thinking. Pushing for clarity prematurely can create frustration. Instead, I suggest letting thoughts emerge and recede naturally, like waves on the shore, rather than clutching at specific conclusions.

People sometimes report feeling isolated or alienated after confronting big questions. That isolation can deepen if there’s no community or support network. Finding peer groups or online forums dedicated to psychedelic integration can help you realize you’re not alone. Sharing your experience - and hearing others’ - normalizes the process and offers new perspectives.

Sometimes, existential questioning reveals unresolved trauma or suppressed emotions. When that floodgate opens, it’s vital to have supportive resources, whether therapy, somatic practices, or trusted friends. The work of Bessel van der Kolk, especially illustrated in The Body Keeps the Score, offers excellent guidance on how to manage trauma’s bodily imprint, which can surface in psychedelic sessions as intense sensations or emotional waves.

Finally, impatience can sabotage the process. Our culture prizes quick fixes, but existential questions are often slow burners. They require time and repeated reflection. Learning patience with yourself and the unfolding mystery keeps you from giving up too soon. The journey after the journey is where real meaning shows up.

6. Integration with Daily Life - Long-Term Perspective

The real test of working with existential questions isn’t during the journey itself but in the days, weeks, and months afterward. Integration is where you translate those huge insights into your everyday life - your relationships, work, values, and habits. It’s how you make the invisible visible and start living out what you discovered.

I like to think of integration as a gradual weaving rather than a sudden switch. The existential questions you faced don’t disappear; instead, they become a quiet background hum that guides your choices. For example, after a session where I confronted questions about purpose, I found myself less willing to tolerate meaningless routines or superficial conversations. I reexamined how I was spending my time, seeking more authenticity and connection.

Daily practices such as journaling help maintain this connection. Returning regularly to your reflections keeps the questions alive without overwhelming you. If you want a special place for this practice, a journal like this Journaling Notebook Leather Bound can inspire commitment and create a sacred space just for you. Writing doesn’t have to be perfect or deep - sometimes just getting words down helps your subconscious untangle complex feelings.

Beyond journaling, mindfulness and meditation deepen your ability to sit with uncertainty and complexity. They develop patience and resilience, which are essential when you’re living with big, open-ended questions. These practices also enhance your body awareness, grounding you when existential anxieties arise unexpectedly.

Community is another key piece. Talking with others who have grappled with similar questions can offer new insights and prevent the isolation that so often accompanies existential thinking. Psychedelic support groups, spiritual communities, or even philosophical reading groups can provide this connection.

Finally, embodying the lessons you’ve learned may mean making concrete changes. Maybe you shift your career toward something more meaningful, or you set boundaries that protect your peace of mind. Integration means allowing your experience to shape your values and actions rather than compartmentalizing the psychedelic journey as “separate” from daily life.

7. Closing Reflection - Sitting with the Question Itself

Here’s a thought that’s stayed with me long after countless journeys: sometimes the question itself is the answer. Not in the way that “meaning” can be neatly packaged, but in the sense that holding the question with openness and presence transforms how we relate to life. Those existential queries - Who am I? What is my purpose? What happens after death? - invite a kind of living mystery rather than a final solution.

In my experience, the urge to resolve these questions quickly can trap us in anxiety or rigid thinking. Instead, sitting with the unknown, letting it be what it is, cultivates a remarkable freedom. It’s like learning to rest in a dark room without needing to turn on every light or finding comfort in a silence that isn’t empty but full of possibility.

That space beyond certainty is where growth happens. It’s also where compassion for ourselves and others can deepen. Recognizing that everyone is grappling with these questions - each in their own way and time - invites humility and connection.

So much of psychedelic therapy’s promise lies in this invitation to live not beyond questions but alongside them, allowing life to unfold without rigid answers. As Michael Pollan describes in How to Change Your Mind, the psychedelic experience can open our eyes to the vastness of existence, not to reduce it, but to deepen our appreciation of its mystery.

Next time you find yourself face-to-face with an existential question during a journey, try not to run or solve. Instead, breathe into it, listen, and let it teach you. Sometimes the best way forward is not through finding an answer but through learning how to live with the question itself.
